content.assessment: This proposal directly engages the equity-relevant finding that younger children are most harmed by long commutes (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-10), and structurally protects the age group most vulnerable to the documented harms of rural travel while relocating the burden to older students who are somewhat better positioned to travel. However, this is explicitly a relocation of sorting rather than its elimination: village-based lower grades preserve early access equity but consolidated upper-grade schools still concentrate students by settlement of origin, and does not address whether teacher quality and multigrade instruction (also linked to shortage patterns, F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-09) can be delivered equitably in the retained lower grades.
opportunities: Could be paired with targeted teacher-training investment for multigrade lower-grade village classrooms to address the documented rural teacher-quality gap (F-live-equity_and_source_mobility-09 [sic])
Grade-split structure creates a natural point to evaluate segregation patterns separately at each stage, rather than treating village schooling as monolithic
strengths: Explicitly targets the developmental stage (grades 1-4) where proximity evidence is strongest against commute harms (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-10)
Preserves some local schooling infrastructure in villages, potentially maintaining partial community anchoring effects relevant to demographic stability (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-01)
threats: Between-settlement segregation persists or could intensify at the consolidated upper-grade level, echoing the ghetto-school concentration dynamic already observed to be worsening (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-05)

If class-size minimums still apply fiscally to the retained lower-grade schools (a general structural pressure independent of this proposal), the equity gains for young children could be undermined regardless of the design
weaknesses: Older students still face the commute burden that disproportionately affects lower-income households (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-10), meaning the equity problem is relocated by age rather than solved

Retained lower-grade village schools remain exposed to the rural teacher shortage and higher proportion of unqualified teachers documented in disadvantaged schools (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-09), without a stated mechanism to address staffing quality
